Modern observability platforms (Prometheus, Grafana, Datadog, Zabbix) aggregate metrics from network devices, servers, and applications into dashboards and alerting systems. The art of alerting is avoiding false positives (which cause alert fatigue and lead to ignored alerts) while catching real problems before they affect users. Alert on symptoms (user-facing impact: error rates, latency) rather than causes (CPU utilization, link utilization) to reduce noise. Troubleshooting network problems requires systematic methodology: define the problem precisely, gather data (pings, traceroutes, packet captures, log analysis), form a hypothesis, test it, and either confirm or reject it before trying the next hypothesis.
key_concepts: SNMP: protocol for monitoring and managing network devices; management station sends GET/SET requests to agents on devices; agents store data in MIBs (Management Information Bases) organized as a tree of OIDs (Object Identifiers)

SNMP versions: v1 (community string authentication, plaintext), v2c (bulk operations, still plaintext), v3 (authentication and encryption, required for security); SNMP traps: unsolicited alerts from devices to the management station

NetFlow/IPFIX: routers export flow records (source/destination IP, ports, protocol, byte/packet counts, timestamps) to a collector; enables traffic analysis, capacity planning, anomaly detection, and billing

Monitoring metrics: availability (device up/down, interface status), utilization (bandwidth, CPU, memory), latency (round-trip time, one-way delay), error rates (packet loss, CRC errors, drops), throughput (actual data transfer rate)

Prometheus and Grafana: Prometheus scrapes metrics from exporters (SNMP exporter, node exporter) and stores time-series data; Grafana visualizes metrics in dashboards and supports alerting based on PromQL queries

Alerting strategy: alert on user-facing symptoms (error rate > 1%, latency > 500ms), not causes (CPU > 80%); use multiple severity levels (critical: page on-call, warning: create ticket, info: log); suppress alerts during maintenance windows

Troubleshooting methodology: (1) define the problem precisely ('users in building A cannot access the intranet' not 'the network is broken'), (2) gather data (ping, traceroute, interface status, error counters), (3) isolate the fault domain (layer, device, link), (4) form and test hypotheses, (5) implement and verify the fix, (6) document the root cause and prevention steps

Common network problems: misconfigured subnet masks (devices cannot communicate despite being on the same network), DNS resolution failures (devices can ping by IP but not by name), VLAN misconfiguration (devices in wrong VLAN), MTU mismatch (large packets dropped), routing loops (packets circulate until TTL expires)

Capacity planning: tracking utilization trends over time, projecting when links will reach capacity, planning upgrades before congestion causes performance degradation; the 70% utilization rule (plan upgrades when sustained utilization exceeds 70%)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Set up network monitoring: (1) Install SNMP on a router/switch (or use a simulated device) and configure community strings. (2) Use snmpwalk to browse the device's MIB and retrieve interface status, uptime, and packet counters. (3) Set up Prometheus with the SNMP exporter to collect metrics automatically every 30 seconds. (4) Create a Grafana dashboard showing: device availability (up/down), interface bandwidth utilization over time, error rate graph, and latency measurement. (5) Configure alerting: alert when interface utilization exceeds 80% for 5 minutes, alert when packet loss exceeds 1%, page when a critical device is unreachable for 2 minutes. (6) Troubleshooting exercise: diagnose a connectivity problem using a systematic approach (check physical layer, check data link, check network layer, check transport layer, check application layer).
setup_requirements: SNMP-enabled network device or net-snmp simulator
Prometheus and Grafana (Docker: docker-compose with prometheus and grafana images)
snmpwalk and snmpget command-line tools
Wireshark for capturing SNMP packets

review.value: Alert when users are affected: error rates up, latency increased, service unavailable. Avoid alerting on causes (CPU > 80%) because high CPU might be normal during peak hours and low CPU does not mean the service is healthy. Symptoms are what matter to users.

When troubleshooting, ask 'why' repeatedly to find the root cause. Network down -> why? Router interface down -> why? CRC errors on the link -> why? Damaged cable -> why? Cable run through a door hinge. Fix the root cause (reroute the cable), not the symptom.

SNMP v1 and v2c use community strings (essentially passwords) transmitted in plaintext. Anyone capturing traffic can read the community string and query/modify network devices. Always use SNMP v3 with authentication and encryption in production.

Track bandwidth utilization trends over months. If a link is at 60% utilization and growing 5% per month, you have 2 months before it hits 70% (time to plan an upgrade) and 8 months before saturation. Proactive capacity planning prevents performance crises.

learning_objectives: Trace a complete network communication from application layer to physical layer and back
Synthesize knowledge from all layers to diagnose complex network problems
Evaluate network design decisions considering performance, security, reliability, and cost
Prepare for professional networking roles with awareness of current trends and emerging technologies
readings: Review all course materials, lecture notes, and lab exercises
Kurose & Ross, Ch. 1 (revisit with full-stack understanding)

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session integrates everything we have covered across the semester by tracing a complete network communication from start to finish, showing how each layer contributes to the successful delivery of a single web page request. When you type 'https://www.example.com' into your browser and press Enter, a remarkable sequence of events occurs involving every layer of the network stack. The browser checks its DNS cache; if no entry is found, the operating system's DNS resolver queries a recursive DNS server, which may query root, TLD, and authoritative DNS servers to resolve the domain to an IP address. The browser then initiates a TCP connection with a three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK), adding one round-trip time of latency. On top of the TCP connection, TLS negotiates encryption with another handshake (ClientHello, ServerHello, key exchange, certificate verification). Only then does the browser send the HTTP GET request. The request data flows down the stack: the HTTP message becomes a TCP segment (with port numbers), the TCP segment becomes an IP packet (with IP addresses), and the IP packet becomes one or more Ethernet frames (with MAC addresses) as it traverses each link on the path. At each router, the Ethernet frame is stripped, the IP destination is looked up in the routing table (longest prefix match), a new Ethernet frame is created for the next link, and the packet is forwarded. The response follows the reverse path back. Understanding this complete flow, from keypress to rendered page, is the hallmark of a network engineer.
key_concepts: Full-stack trace: DNS resolution -> TCP handshake -> TLS handshake -> HTTP request -> IP routing -> Ethernet switching -> physical transmission -> reverse path for response; each step adds latency and introduces potential failure points

Cross-layer interactions: TCP congestion control responds to network-layer congestion; application-layer HTTP/2 multiplexing mitigates transport-layer head-of-line blocking; physical-layer errors trigger data-link retransmission

Network design trade-offs: performance (minimize latency, maximize throughput) vs security (encryption adds processing overhead, firewalls add hop count) vs reliability (redundancy costs money) vs cost (higher bandwidth links are more expensive)

Troubleshooting with layers: when something breaks, identify which layer is failing; can you ping the IP (network layer works)? Can you connect to the port (transport layer works)? Does the HTTP response return correctly (application layer works)? Narrow down systematically.

Modern networking trends: HTTP/3 and QUIC (UDP-based transport), 5G and edge computing, zero-trust networking (verify every connection, not just the perimeter), network automation and intent-based networking, AI-driven network management

Professional certifications: CCNA (Cisco networking fundamentals), CompTIA Network+ (vendor-neutral networking), AWS Solutions Architect (cloud networking), JNCIA (Juniper networking); certifications validate practical knowledge

Career paths: network engineer (design and maintain network infrastructure), cloud architect (design cloud networking and security), site reliability engineer (maintain service availability), security engineer (protect network infrastructure and data)
Continuing education: networking evolves continuously; new protocols (QUIC, WireGuard), new paradigms (zero trust, service mesh), and new technologies (Wi-Fi 7, 5G-Advanced) require ongoing learning
lecture_notes.demonstration: Complete end-to-end trace: (1) Open Wireshark and navigate to an HTTPS website. (2) Identify every protocol in the capture: DNS query/response, TCP three-way handshake, TLS 1.3 handshake, HTTP/2 request/response. (3) For each protocol, identify the layer, the addresses/identifiers used, and the purpose. (4) Calculate the total time from DNS query to rendered page, breaking it down by phase: DNS resolution time, TCP handshake time, TLS handshake time, HTTP request-response time. (5) Troubleshooting challenge: given a network topology diagram with a reported connectivity problem, systematically diagnose the issue by testing at each layer using ping (network), telnet (transport), and curl (application).
setup_requirements: Wireshark for complete protocol analysis
Network topology diagram for troubleshooting exercise
Review materials from all previous sessions
Practice problems covering all layers

review.value: No single layer provides the complete communication service. Application protocols define what to communicate, transport protocols define how to deliver it reliably, network protocols define how to route it across networks, and link/physical protocols define how to transmit it across individual links. Each layer depends on the layers below.

A web page load involves: DNS (0-100ms, cached or not), TCP handshake (1 RTT, ~20-100ms), TLS handshake (1 RTT for TLS 1.3), HTTP request-response (1+ RTT plus server processing). Total: 3-4 RTTs minimum. Reducing RTT (via CDN) or eliminating handshakes (via keep-alive, 0-RTT) are the most impactful optimizations.

Physical: locked server rooms, cable management. Data link: 802.1X port authentication, MAC filtering. Network: firewalls, ACLs. Transport: TLS encryption. Application: authentication, input validation. Security must be applied at every layer because attackers target the weakest one.

Specific technologies change (new WiFi standards, new cloud services, new protocols), but fundamentals endure: layered architecture, addressing and routing, reliability mechanisms, security principles, and troubleshooting methodology. Master the fundamentals and you can learn any new technology quickly.

quizzes.topic: Network Models, Physical Layer, and Data Link Layer

questions.question: A channel has a bandwidth of 6 MHz and a signal-to-noise ratio of 20 dB. Calculate the maximum theoretical data rate using Shannon's theorem. Show your work including the conversion from dB to linear scale.
questions.answer:
Step 1: Convert SNR from dB to linear: SNR(linear) = 10^(SNR_dB/10) = 10^(20/10) = 10^2 = 100

Step 2: Apply Shannon's theorem: C = B * log2(1 + S/N) = 6,000,000 * log2(1 + 100) = 6,000,000 * log2(101)

Step 3: Calculate log2(101) = ln(101)/ln(2) = 4.615/0.693 = 6.66 bits

Step 4: C = 6,000,000 * 6.66 = 39.96 Mbps

The maximum theoretical data rate is approximately 39.96 Mbps.

rubric.key: Correct dB to linear conversion: 10^(20/10) = 100
Correct application of Shannon's formula with all values
Correct final answer approximately 39.96 Mbps with units

questions.question: Explain the CRC error detection process. Given data bits 1101011 and generator polynomial 10011, perform the CRC calculation and show the transmitted data with the CRC appended.
questions.answer:
CRC process: (1) Append (generator length - 1) = 4 zeros to the data: 11010110000. (2) Divide by generator 10011 using XOR division.

11010110000 / 10011:
11010 XOR 10011 = 01001 -> bring down 1 -> 10011
10011 XOR 10011 = 00000 -> bring down 1 -> 00001
00001 -> bring down 0 -> 00010
00010 -> bring down 0 -> 00100
00100 -> bring down 0 -> 01000

Wait, let me redo carefully:
11010110000
10011
------
01001110000
 10011
 ------
 00001010000
    10011
    ------
    00001100
      10011
      -----
      01111
       1111 (remainder = 4 bits)

Remainder: 1110
Transmitted data: 1101011 + 1110 = 11010111110

The receiver divides 11010111110 by 10011. If the remainder is 0000, no error is detected.

rubric.key: Correctly appends 4 zeros to the data before division
Correct XOR polynomial long division steps
Correct CRC remainder and transmitted data

questions.question: Compare CSMA/CD (used in Ethernet) and CSMA/CA (used in WiFi). Why can wireless networks not use collision detection? How does CSMA/CA handle the problem?
questions.answer:
CSMA/CD (wired Ethernet): a device listens to the medium (carrier sense), transmits if idle, and monitors the medium during transmission. If a collision is detected (the received signal differs from the transmitted signal), the device stops transmitting, sends a jam signal, and waits a random exponential backoff before retrying.

Wireless networks cannot use collision detection because: (1) A wireless device's own transmission signal is millions of times stronger than any incoming signal, making it impossible to detect collisions while transmitting (the transmitted signal drowns out everything else). (2) The hidden terminal problem means collisions may occur at the receiver but not at the sender.

CSMA/CA (WiFi): listen before transmitting, wait for DIFS + random backoff, transmit the complete frame, wait for an ACK from the receiver. If no ACK is received (indicating a collision or loss), retransmit after a longer random backoff. Optional RTS/CTS handshake reserves the channel before data transmission, solving the hidden terminal problem.

rubric.key: Correctly explains CSMA/CD with collision detection and jam signal
Explains why collision detection is impossible in wireless (signal strength disparity)
Explains CSMA/CA with ACK-based collision recovery and optional RTS/CTS

questions.question: Given the network 172.16.0.0/16, design a VLSM addressing scheme for three departments: Engineering (200 hosts), Marketing (50 hosts), and Executive (10 hosts). For each subnet, provide the network address, subnet mask, broadcast address, first usable host, and last usable host.
questions.answer:
Allocate subnets largest first to minimize address waste:

Engineering (200 hosts): needs at least 201 addresses (200 + network + broadcast). Next power of 2: 256 = 2^8, so /24.
  Network: 172.16.0.0/24
  Subnet mask: 255.255.255.0
  Broadcast: 172.16.0.255
  First usable: 172.16.0.1
  Last usable: 172.16.0.254
  Usable hosts: 254

Marketing (50 hosts): needs 52 addresses. Next power of 2: 64 = 2^6, so /26.
  Network: 172.16.1.0/26
  Subnet mask: 255.255.255.192
  Broadcast: 172.16.1.63
  First usable: 172.16.1.1
  Last usable: 172.16.1.62
  Usable hosts: 62

Executive (10 hosts): needs 12 addresses. Next power of 2: 16 = 2^4, so /28.
  Network: 172.16.1.64/28
  Subnet mask: 255.255.255.240
  Broadcast: 172.16.1.79
  First usable: 172.16.1.65
  Last usable: 172.16.1.78
  Usable hosts: 14

rubric.key: Correct prefix lengths for each department based on host requirements
Correct network and broadcast addresses for all three subnets
Correct first and last usable host addresses
Subnets do not overlap and are allocated largest-first for VLSM

questions.question: Explain the count-to-infinity problem in distance-vector routing using a concrete 3-router example. Describe how split horizon with poison reverse mitigates the problem.
questions.answer:
Example: Routers A, B, C in a line. A--B cost 1, B--C cost 1. All routers know: distance to C from A = 2 (via B), from B = 1 (direct).

The B-C link fails. B sets distance to C = infinity. Before B's update reaches A, A sends its distance vector to B: 'I can reach C with cost 2.' B thinks: I can reach C through A with cost 1 (A-B link) + 2 (A's distance to C) = 3. B updates its distance to C to 3. A then receives B's update and computes: cost to C = 1 (A-B) + 3 (B's distance) = 4. This continues, incrementing by 2 each iteration, until reaching infinity (16 in RIP).

Split horizon with poison reverse: when B learns its route to C via the direct link, it advertises distance to C = infinity to A (poison reverse: tell your neighbor that the route it gave you costs infinity). When the B-C link fails, A already has no alternative route through B, so it does not offer a false route back. The loop is broken immediately.

rubric.key: Concrete 3-router example showing the looping behavior step by step
Explains split horizon: do not advertise a route back through the neighbor you learned it from
Explains poison reverse: advertise infinity for routes learned from a neighbor

questions.question: How does a switch learn MAC addresses? Describe the process when a switch receives a frame with an unknown destination MAC address and what happens differently on subsequent frames to the same destination.
questions.answer:
When a switch receives a frame:

1. Learning: The switch records the source MAC address and the port it arrived on in its MAC address table, with an aging timer (typically 300 seconds). Every frame contributes to the switch's knowledge of which devices are on which ports.

2. Unknown destination: If the destination MAC is not in the table, the switch floods the frame — it sends a copy out every port except the one the frame arrived on. This ensures the frame reaches the destination regardless of where it is.

3. Known destination: When the destination device responds, the switch learns its MAC-to-port mapping from the response frame's source MAC. For subsequent frames to the same destination, the switch forwards the frame only to the specific port where the destination is connected (unicast forwarding), not to all ports.

Result: After the first exchange, the switch knows both MAC addresses and their ports, and all subsequent communication between these two devices is forwarded only on the relevant ports, not flooded.

rubric.key: Explains source MAC learning with port association and aging timer
Explains flooding for unknown destinations
Explains unicast forwarding after learning and the efficiency improvement

questions.question: Explain TCP's three-way handshake step by step with sequence numbers. Then explain why a two-way handshake would be insufficient for reliable connection establishment.
questions.answer:
Three-way handshake:
1. SYN: Client sends a segment with SYN flag set and initial sequence number X (e.g., ISN=1000). This means 'I want to connect, my starting sequence number is 1000.'

2. SYN-ACK: Server responds with SYN and ACK flags set, acknowledging X+1 (ACK=1001, meaning 'I received your SYN') and sending its own initial sequence number Y (e.g., ISN=5000).

3. ACK: Client sends ACK for Y+1 (ACK=5001, meaning 'I received your SYN'). Connection is established; data transfer can begin.

A two-way handshake would be insufficient because:
- After step 1 and 2, the server has confirmed it can reach the client, but the client has not confirmed it received the server's sequence number.
- Old duplicate SYN segments from previous connections could cause the server to establish a connection that the client never requested (the client would not send the third ACK for a stale SYN). The three-way handshake prevents half-open connections from stale segments.

rubric.key: Correctly describes all three steps with SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK and sequence numbers
Explains why two-way is insufficient: bidirectional sequence number agreement needed
Mentions stale SYN prevention or half-open connection problem

questions.question: A DNS resolver receives a query for mail.cs.stanford.edu. Describe the iterative resolution process, listing each server queried and the response received at each step.
questions.answer:
1. Client queries local DNS resolver for mail.cs.stanford.edu.

2. Resolver queries a root DNS server. Root server responds: 'I don't know, but here are the .edu TLD servers' (returns NS records for .edu TLD).

3. Resolver queries the .edu TLD server. TLD server responds: 'I don't know, but here are the authoritative servers for stanford.edu' (returns NS records like ns1.stanford.edu).

4. Resolver queries the stanford.edu authoritative server for mail.cs.stanford.edu. The authoritative server may delegate to cs.stanford.edu if a subdomain zone exists, or it may directly return the A record with the IP address.

5. Resolver caches the result with the TTL from the response and returns the IP address to the client.

At each step, the resolver follows referrals from one server to the next, performing the queries itself (iterative resolution). The client made one query; the resolver made 3-4 queries on its behalf.

rubric.key: Correct order: root, TLD (.edu), authoritative (stanford.edu)
Each step describes the query and the referral response
Mentions caching with TTL and the distinction between iterative and recursive

questions.question: Calculate the bandwidth-delay product for a 1 Gbps link with an RTT of 40 ms. Explain what this means for TCP window size and what happens if the TCP window is smaller than the BDP.
questions.answer:
Bandwidth-Delay Product = Bandwidth x RTT
BDP = 1,000,000,000 bits/sec x 0.040 sec = 40,000,000 bits = 5,000,000 bytes = 5 MB

This means 5 MB of data can be 'in flight' (sent but not yet acknowledged) at any time. The TCP window size must be at least 5 MB to fully utilize the 1 Gbps link.

If the TCP window is smaller than the BDP (e.g., 64 KB default):
- The sender transmits 64 KB, then stops and waits for an ACK (it has filled its window).
- The ACK takes 20 ms (half RTT) to arrive.
- The sender can only send 64 KB per RTT = 64 KB / 0.040 s = 1.6 MB/s = 12.8 Mbps.
- The link is utilized at only 12.8 / 1000 = 1.28% of its capacity.

Solution: TCP window scaling option (RFC 7323) allows window sizes up to 1 GB, enabling full utilization of high-bandwidth, high-latency links.

rubric.key: Correct BDP calculation with units: 5 MB
Explains that TCP window must be >= BDP for full utilization
Shows the underutilization calculation with a small window and mentions TCP window scaling

quizzes.topic: Network Security, Wireless, and Socket Programming

questions.question: Trace the TLS 1.3 handshake step by step. For each message, state who sends it, what it contains, and what it accomplishes. Why does TLS 1.3 require fewer round trips than TLS 1.2?
questions.answer:
TLS 1.3 Handshake (1-RTT):

1. ClientHello (Client -> Server): Contains supported cipher suites, supported key exchange groups, and a key share (the client's Diffie-Hellman public value). The client guesses which key exchange the server will choose and sends its key share preemptively.

2. ServerHello + EncryptedExtensions + Certificate + CertificateVerify + Finished (Server -> Client): Server selects cipher suite, sends its key share (DH public value), its X.509 certificate, a signature proving it owns the certificate's private key, and a Finished message. Everything after ServerHello is encrypted using the derived handshake keys.

3. Finished (Client -> Server): Client verifies the certificate chain to a trusted CA, verifies the server's signature, derives the session keys, and sends Finished. Application data can now flow encrypted.

TLS 1.3 is 1-RTT vs TLS 1.2's 2-RTT because the client sends its key share in ClientHello (preemptively), so the server can derive encryption keys from the first message. TLS 1.2 required a separate round trip for key exchange. TLS 1.3 also supports 0-RTT for repeat connections (client sends early data with the first message using a pre-shared key from a previous session).

rubric.key: Correctly describes all three steps with sender and content
Explains the preemptive key share reducing round trips
Mentions certificate verification and forward secrecy

questions.question: Write Python pseudocode for a TCP server that accepts connections from multiple clients concurrently using threading. The server should echo received messages back to the client and handle client disconnection gracefully.
questions.answer:
import socket
import threading

def handle_client(client_socket, address):
    print(f'Connected: {address}')
    try:
        while True:
            data = client_socket.recv(4096)
            if not data:  # Client disconnected cleanly
                break
            client_socket.sendall(data)  # Echo back
    except ConnectionResetError:
        print(f'Client {address} disconnected unexpectedly')
    finally:
        client_socket.close()
        print(f'Closed: {address}')

def main():
    server = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
    server.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_REUSEADDR, 1)
    server.bind(('0.0.0.0', 8000))
    server.listen(5)
    print('Server listening on port 8000')
    try:
        while True:
            client_socket, address = server.accept()
            thread = threading.Thread(target=handle_client, args=(client_socket, address))
            thread.daemon = True
            thread.start()
    finally:
        server.close()

main()

rubric.key: Correct socket lifecycle: socket, bind, listen, accept loop
Threading: spawns a thread per client connection
Handles empty recv (clean disconnect) and ConnectionResetError
Uses sendall for complete data transmission and closes sockets in finally

questions.question: A company has deployed IoT temperature sensors using MQTT with QoS 0. Occasionally, sensor readings are missing from the dashboard. Explain why this happens and recommend a solution with trade-offs.
questions.answer:
QoS 0 (at most once) provides no delivery guarantee. The publisher sends the message to the broker without waiting for an acknowledgment. If the message is lost due to network issues (WiFi interference, temporary disconnection, broker overload), it is simply gone. There is no retransmission.

Solution: Upgrade to QoS 1 (at least once). The publisher sends the message and waits for a PUBACK from the broker. If no PUBACK is received within a timeout, the publisher retransmits. This guarantees the message is delivered at least once.

Trade-offs:
- QoS 1 uses more bandwidth (PUBACK for every message) and more battery (radio stays on longer waiting for ACK)
- QoS 1 may deliver duplicate messages (if the PUBACK is lost, the publisher retransmits and the broker delivers twice); the subscriber must handle duplicates (idempotent processing or deduplication)
- QoS 2 (exactly once) eliminates duplicates but requires a 4-message handshake, doubling the overhead; overkill for temperature readings where a duplicate is harmless

Recommendation: QoS 1 with idempotent subscriber processing (overwrite the latest reading, not append). Additionally, enable MQTT retained messages so the dashboard shows the last known reading even after subscriber restart.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies QoS 0 as the cause: no delivery guarantee, no retransmission
Recommends QoS 1 with clear explanation of the acknowledgment mechanism
Discusses trade-offs: bandwidth, battery, potential duplicates, and mitigation

questions.question: Design a VPC architecture for a web application with a public-facing web tier, a private application tier, and a private database tier. Specify subnets, security group rules, and routing for each tier. Explain why the database should not have a public IP address.
questions.answer:
VPC: 10.0.0.0/16

Public subnet (web tier): 10.0.1.0/24
  - Route table: 0.0.0.0/0 -> Internet Gateway
  - Security group: Allow inbound HTTP (80) and HTTPS (443) from 0.0.0.0/0, SSH (22) from admin IP only
  - Hosts: Application Load Balancer, bastion host

Private subnet (app tier): 10.0.2.0/24
  - Route table: 0.0.0.0/0 -> NAT Gateway (for outbound updates only)
  - Security group: Allow inbound from web-tier security group on port 3000 (app port), SSH from bastion security group
  - Hosts: Application servers

Private subnet (database tier): 10.0.3.0/24
  - Route table: no Internet route (fully isolated)
  - Security group: Allow inbound PostgreSQL (5432) only from app-tier security group
  - Hosts: RDS database instances

The database should not have a public IP because:
1. Attack surface: A public IP exposes the database to brute-force attacks, SQL injection scanning, and exploitation of any database server vulnerabilities.
2. Defense in depth: The database should only be reachable through the application tier, which validates and sanitizes all input.
3. Compliance: PCI DSS, HIPAA, and SOC 2 require that sensitive data stores are not directly accessible from the Internet.

rubric.key: Three-tier architecture with correct subnet types (public, private, private)
Security group rules using security group references (not IP addresses) between tiers
Correct routing (IGW for public, NAT for app, no Internet for database)
Clear explanation of why database needs no public IP (attack surface, defense in depth)

questions.question: Your monitoring system shows that a core router's CPU utilization is at 95%. Should you immediately alert the on-call engineer? Explain your reasoning and describe what additional data you would gather before escalating.
questions.answer:
No, high CPU alone should not trigger an immediate page. CPU utilization is a cause metric, not a symptom metric. A router at 95% CPU might be functioning perfectly if it is processing traffic within normal parameters. Alerting on causes creates alert fatigue.

Before escalating, I would check:
1. User-facing symptoms: Are there packet drops, increased latency, or routing convergence delays? If users are not affected, the high CPU is not an incident.
2. Historical baseline: Is 95% CPU normal for this router during peak hours? Compare against the same time on previous days/weeks.
3. Traffic volume: Is there a legitimate traffic spike (a popular event, a software update rollout) that explains the increased CPU?
4. Process breakdown: Which processes are consuming CPU? If it is routing protocol processing (OSPF SPF calculation) after a topology change, it is temporary. If it is control-plane policing from a DDoS, that is different.
5. Error counters: Are there input/output queue drops? Increasing queue drops with high CPU indicates the router cannot keep up with traffic.

I would alert if: user-facing metrics are degraded (packet loss >0.1%, latency >2x baseline), or if CPU has been above 95% for >15 minutes with increasing queue drops.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies that CPU is a cause metric, not a symptom — should not page alone
Lists at least 3 specific additional data points to gather
Defines clear criteria for when to escalate (symptom-based thresholds)

questions.question: Explain the difference between SDN's control plane and data plane. What problem does separating them solve that traditional networking cannot easily address?
questions.answer:
Data plane: the hardware or software that actually forwards packets based on rules in a forwarding table. It operates at wire speed, examining packet headers and taking actions (forward to port, drop, modify header). This is what switches and routers do for every packet.

Control plane: the logic that decides what rules go into the forwarding table. It runs routing algorithms, processes routing protocol messages, and populates forwarding tables. In traditional networking, the control plane runs on each individual device.

In traditional networking, each device runs its own control plane independently. This creates problems:
1. Configuration consistency: Ensuring 1000 switches have consistent security policies requires configuring each one individually (error-prone, slow).
2. Network-wide optimization: No device has a global view. Each router optimizes locally, which may not produce globally optimal paths.
3. Rapid response: When a link fails, convergence depends on distributed protocol interaction (potentially slow).

SDN separates the control plane into a centralized controller that has a global view of the network. The controller computes forwarding rules and programs all data plane devices via OpenFlow or similar protocols. This enables:
- Centralized policy management (one place to define security rules for the entire network)
- Global traffic optimization (the controller sees all links and can balance load optimally)
- Programmability (applications request network services via the controller's API, enabling automation)

rubric.key: Correctly defines data plane (forwarding) and control plane (decision-making)
Explains the problem with distributed control in traditional networking
Explains benefits of centralized control: consistency, global optimization, programmability

major_assignments.title: Reliable File Transfer Protocol over UDP
major_assignments.description: Design and implement a reliable file transfer protocol on top of UDP. Your protocol must handle packet loss, reordering, and corruption by implementing sequence numbers, acknowledgments, retransmission timers, and checksums. Implement both stop-and-wait and sliding window (Go-Back-N or Selective Repeat) variants. Test over a simulated lossy network with configurable loss rate. Analyze and compare protocol performance under varying conditions.
objectives: Implement reliable delivery mechanisms on top of an unreliable transport protocol
Design a protocol with well-defined message formats, state machines, and error handling
Compare stop-and-wait and sliding window performance empirically
Analyze protocol behavior under varying network conditions (loss rate, RTT, bandwidth)
deliverables: Source code for sender and receiver implementing both stop-and-wait and sliding window protocols
Protocol specification document describing message formats, state machines, and error handling procedures
Network simulator that introduces configurable packet loss, delay, and reordering
Performance analysis report: throughput vs loss rate graph for both protocols at 0%, 1%, 5%, 10%, 20% loss rates
Test results demonstrating successful file transfer (files match byte-for-byte) under all test conditions

criteria: Protocol specification clearly defines message formats with header fields (sequence number, ack number, checksum, flags, data length)
State machines for sender and receiver are well-defined with all transitions and edge cases
Sequence numbers and acknowledgments handle all scenarios: loss, duplication, reordering
Checksum calculation and verification detect corruption reliably
Both stop-and-wait and sliding window variants are correctly implemented
Files transfer correctly with no byte errors under all loss rates (verified with checksums)
Retransmission timer adapts to network conditions or is tuned based on measured RTT
Protocol handles edge cases: duplicate packets, out-of-order delivery, connection reset, large files
Throughput measured at 5 different loss rates for both protocol variants
Clear graphs comparing stop-and-wait vs sliding window performance
Analysis explains why sliding window outperforms stop-and-wait and by how much
Window size tuning: experiments with different window sizes showing the effect on throughput
Protocol specification is clear enough for another developer to implement a compatible client/server
Performance analysis is presented with properly labeled graphs
Code is well-documented with clear function names and comments on non-obvious logic
README includes build instructions, usage examples, and test methodology
Correct reliable file transfer over lossy UDP with both stop-and-wait and sliding window
Well-designed protocol with clear specification suitable for independent implementation
Performance analysis demonstrating and explaining the advantage of sliding window
Thorough testing under multiple loss conditions with byte-accurate file verification

major_assignments.title: Network Application: Multi-Client Chat System
major_assignments.description: Design and implement a multi-client chat system using TCP sockets. The system must support user registration, private messaging, group channels, and message history. Implement a custom application-layer protocol on top of TCP with proper framing, error handling, and a documented specification. The server must handle at least 20 concurrent clients efficiently.
objectives: Design an application-layer protocol with defined message types, framing, and error responses
Build a concurrent TCP server that handles many simultaneous client connections
Implement proper network error handling: disconnections, timeouts, and malformed messages
Test the application under concurrent load to verify correctness and scalability
deliverables: Client application with command-line or simple GUI interface
Protocol specification document (message types, framing format, state diagram, error codes)
Load test results showing server behavior with 5, 10, and 20 concurrent clients
Wireshark capture analysis showing protocol messages in action

criteria: Protocol defines clear message types (LOGIN, MSG, PRIVMSG, JOIN, LEAVE, LIST, ERROR) with field specifications
Framing protocol (length prefix or delimiter) handles variable-length messages correctly
Error codes and responses are well-defined for all failure cases
Protocol specification is detailed enough for independent implementation
Server handles 20+ concurrent clients without blocking or crashing
Broadcast messages reach all clients in a channel, private messages reach only the target
Client disconnection is handled gracefully (removed from channels, other clients notified)
Server validates all input and responds with appropriate error codes for invalid requests
Client sends and receives messages concurrently (not blocking on input or output)
Client displays received messages with sender identification and timestamps
Client handles server disconnection gracefully with reconnection or clean exit
Commands are intuitive (/msg user, /join channel, /list, /quit)
Load test with 5, 10, and 20 clients showing correct message delivery
Wireshark capture showing at least 3 different message types in the custom protocol
Documentation covers setup, usage, protocol specification, and known limitations
Edge cases tested: simultaneous messages, rapid connect/disconnect, long messages
Working multi-client chat system with custom application-layer protocol
Server handles 20+ concurrent clients with proper error handling and graceful disconnection
Protocol specification is clear, complete, and demonstrated with Wireshark captures
Load testing verifies correctness and performance under concurrent usage

major_assignments.title: Cloud Network Architecture Design and Security Audit
major_assignments.description: Design a production-ready cloud network architecture for a multi-tier web application serving 10,000 concurrent users. Deploy the architecture using Infrastructure as Code (Terraform or CloudFormation). Conduct a security audit of the deployed infrastructure, identifying and remediating vulnerabilities. Document the architecture, security findings, and remediation steps.
objectives: Design a multi-tier VPC architecture with proper security zones and network segmentation
Deploy cloud infrastructure using Infrastructure as Code with version-controlled configuration
Conduct a network security audit identifying misconfigurations and vulnerabilities
Apply cloud networking best practices for scalability, security, and cost optimization
deliverables: Architecture diagram showing VPC, subnets, security groups, load balancers, NAT gateways, and routing
Infrastructure as Code files (Terraform or CloudFormation) defining all network resources
Security audit report identifying at least 5 potential vulnerabilities with severity ratings
Remediation documentation showing before/after configurations for each security finding
Cost estimate for the architecture at 10,000 concurrent users

criteria: Multi-AZ deployment for high availability with proper subnet placement across availability zones
Three-tier architecture: public (load balancer), private (application), private (database) with correct routing
Security groups reference each other (not IP addresses) for inter-tier communication
NAT Gateway for private subnet outbound access, no unnecessary public IP addresses
All network resources defined in IaC (VPC, subnets, route tables, security groups, NAT, IGW, ALB)
IaC is modular and parameterized (CIDR ranges, instance types, environment name as variables)
Resources have descriptive names and tags for cost tracking and operational clarity
IaC applies successfully and creates a functional network (terraform apply succeeds)
At least 5 security findings identified with severity (critical, high, medium, low)
Each finding includes: description, risk explanation, affected resources, and remediation steps
Remediation is implemented and verified (before/after security group rules, network ACLs)
Audit covers: overly permissive security groups, public access to private resources, encryption in transit, logging
Architecture diagram is clear, accurate, and includes all network components
Design decisions are justified (why this subnet size, why this security group configuration)
Cost estimate is realistic and includes all billable network resources
Documentation enables another engineer to understand, deploy, and maintain the architecture
Production-quality multi-tier VPC architecture with proper security zones
All infrastructure defined as code and successfully deployed
Security audit identifies real vulnerabilities with implemented remediations
Comprehensive documentation covering architecture, security, and cost

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures and mandatory for lab sessions where packet capture and network configuration exercises are conducted. More than 3 unexcused absences may result in a reduced participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late. Protocol implementation assignments require working code; partial credit is given for correct protocol design with incomplete implementation.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of networking concepts is encouraged. All code, protocol designs, and analysis must be individual work unless pair programming is explicitly assigned. Discussing approaches is fine; sharing code or protocol specifications is not.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Copying protocol implementations from the Internet, other students, or AI tools without attribution constitutes plagiarism. AI tools may be used as learning aids but all submitted work must be understood and explainable by the student.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a Linux environment (native, WSL, or VM), Wireshark, Python 3.12+ or C with socket libraries, and network access for packet capture experiments. A cloud provider free tier account (AWS, Azure, or GCP) is needed for the cloud networking assignment. Lab computers with all tools are available.
